text: The laparoscopic cholecystectomy begins with the insertion of an 11mm camera port for the telescope, along with three additional ports: one in the epigastrium and two 5mm ports in the right hypochondrium at the mid-clavicular and mid-axillary lines. The mid-axillary port is used for fundal retraction, and the operation starts with posterior Calot's triangle dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection using a hook cautery or Maryland forceps, ensuring proper hemostasis. The anterior dissection follows, separating tissue layers in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects Calot's triangle, identifying the cystic duct and cystic artery while achieving the critical view of safety. The posterior dissection is performed to ensure no structures extend from the gallbladder bed into the liver.

The surgeon visualizes the dissected Calot's triangle, ensuring no structures extend into the liver. The cystic duct and cystic artery are carefully separated using a Maryland dissector with proper hemostasis. Clips are applied, two proximally and one distally, before cutting the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon achieves proper hemostasis using bipolar, unipolar, and harmonic scalpels. After clipping and cutting the cystic duct and artery, the gallbladder is dissected from the liver bed and removed through the epigastric or umbilical port for histopathological examination.
